blocks.speaker: Mr. Cole
blocks.content: You know what I hate more than bad advice?

blocks.speaker: Mr. Cole
blocks.content: Good advice delivered at the wrong time by somebody who can't tell the difference.
He leaned forward, arms on his knees.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Cole
blocks.content: I had a friend like Jonathan once. When I was about your age. Smart. Looked out for me. Had an opinion about everything I should be doing and most of the time he wasn't wrong.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Cole
blocks.content: Nothing dramatic. We just — hit a wall. He kept trying to build my life the way he thought it should look. And I kept letting him because he'd been right enough times that it felt easier than pushing back.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Cole
blocks.content: Eventually I said the thing I should've said earlier and he took it hard. Like it was a judgment on him instead of just — me needing to be me.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Cole
blocks.content: Yeah. Took a little time. But yeah.
He said it simple. Like it was a fact about weather — here's what happened, here's what came after. No lesson attached. No moral. Just: here's what I know about this particular kind of thing.

blocks.prompt: Mr. Cole just told Larry something from his own life. What does Larry take from it?

options.label: That it passes. That the wall is real but it isn't permanent.
That it cost something even when it worked out. That's worth knowing.
